retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal varices are dilated submucosal distal esophageal veins connecting the portal and systemic circulations. They form due to portal hypertension, which commonly is a result of cirrhosis, resistance to portal blood flow, and increased portal venous blood inflow. Variceal rupture is the most common fatal complication of cirrhosis. the severity of liver disease correlates with the presence of varices and risk of bleeding. This activity reviews the evaluation of esophageal varices and the role of the interprofessional team in managing this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Acute esophageal variceal bleeding constitutes a life-threatening complication of portal hypertension, which most commonly arises from cirrhosis. Portal hypertension increases pressure within the portal venous system, leading to the formation of dilated submucosal veins in the distal esophagus, which are susceptible to rupture. Risk factors include chronic liver disease, alcohol use, viral hepatitis, and coagulopathy. Patients typically present with hematemesis, melena, hypotension, tachycardia, and signs of hypovolemic shock. Initial management involves hemodynamic stabilization, vasoactive medications, prophylactic antibiotics, and urgent endoscopic therapy such as band ligation or sclerotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal varices are dilated submucosal distal esophageal veins connecting the portal and systemic circulations. This happens due to portal hypertension (most commonly a result of cirrhosis), resistance to portal blood flow, and increased portal venous blood inflow. The most common fatal complication of cirrhosis is variceal rupture; the severity of liver disease correlates with the presence of varices and risk of bleeding. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.content: Formation of gastric varices after eradication of esophageal varices Esophageal varices can recur. Hepatic encephalopathy, renal dysfunction, hepatorenal syndrome Infections after banding/ligation of varices

retrieved_docs.title: Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal varices are a direct manifestation of chronic portal hypertension, which most commonly results from cirrhosis. Progressive fibrosis, architectural distortion, and regenerative nodules obstruct normal portal venous outflow in the cirrhotic liver. This resistance elevates pressure within the portal system, diverting blood through collateral pathways connecting the portal and systemic circulations. [7] One of the most clinically significant networks involves the left gastric vein (coronary vein), which drains into the portal vein and communicates with the esophageal venous plexus. These submucosal and paraesophageal veins further connect with the systemic azygos and hemiazygos veins, forming fragile, thin-walled varices in the distal esophagus and proximal stomach. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Nadolol -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to its FDA-approved indications, nadolol proves beneficial in various off-label uses, including the treatment of atrial fibrillation, ventricular arrhythmias linked to long QT syndrome, ventricular premature beats, catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, supraventricular tachycardia, prophylaxis against gastroesophageal variceal hemorrhage in liver cirrhosis, thyrotoxicosis management, as well as migraine and vascular headache prophylaxis. This activity reviews indications for nadolol, elucidates its mechanism of action, explores adverse drug reactions and toxicity profiles, delineates management strategies and contraindications, and provides a comprehensive understanding of nadolol's multifaceted role in addressing hypertension, angina, and related conditions. This activity explores nadolol's pharmacological nuances and clinical applications, empowering interprofessional healthcare providers with the expertise to utilize its therapeutic potential effectively in diverse cardiovascular scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Gastric varices are dilated portosystemic collateral blood vessels that develop as a complication of portal hypertension or extrahepatic portal vein obstruction. Based on the location and relation to the esophagus, gastric varices are further classified into gastroesophageal varices isolated gastric varices. Acute bleeding from gastric varices is life-threatening and is associated with higher mortality compared to esophageal variceal bleeding. This activity reviews the etiology, pathophysiology, diagnosis, management of gastric varices and highlights the interprofessional team's role in evaluating and managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Variceal bleeding can occur in patients with an elevated portal vein pressure, defined as >12 mm Hg. Locations of increased resistance of portal blood flow include prehepatic (eg, portal vein thrombosis), intrahepatic (eg, cirrhosis), and posthepatic (eg, hepatic vein or inferior vena cava thrombosis). Increased portal vein pressure leads to the formation of portosystemic collaterals, a complex process of gradual dilation and hypertrophy of preexisting vascular channels called varices. [14] [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology and pathophysiology of gastric varices. Summarize the diagnostic evaluation of gastric varices. Describe the management of gastric varices, Review interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to improve outcomes in patients with gastric varices. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal Hypertension -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of portal hypertension frequently requires interprofessional consultation with several specialists, including gastroenterology, hepatology, and nephrology.

retrieved_docs.title: Portacaval Shunt -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: An emergent esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is crucial in the diagnosis and therapy for a suspected acute variceal bleed. Its usefulness is in the identification of the presence of varices as well as an exclusion for other sources of bleeding, for example, gastritis, ulcers, portal hypertensive gastropathy. Endoscopic interventions are effective in controlling hemorrhage and almost 90% of patients including sclerotherapy or variceal band ligation. If this fails, luminal tamponade with Sengstaken-Blakemore or Minnesota tubes can be utilized, both of which have large round gastric balloons that can be used to compress the GE junction and decrease blood flow to esophageal varices when placed on traction with a distal port to allow for the evacuation of luminal contents within the stomach. You wary of aspiration pneumonia as it is the most common complication of luminal tamponade, but elective endotracheal intubation is recommended as it can prevent aspiration especially in patients who have encephalopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Portal hypertension causes portocaval anastomosis to develop to decompress portal circulation. Normal portal pressure is between 5-10 mmHg but in the presence of portal obstruction, the pressure may be as high as 15-20 mmHg. Since the portal venous system has no valves, resistance at any level between the splanchnic vessels and right side of the heart results in retrograde flow and elevated pressure. The collaterals slowly enlarge and connect the systemic circulation to the portal venous system. Over time, this leads to a congested submucosal venous plexus with tortuous dilated veins in the distal esophagus. Variceal rupture results in hemorrhage. Pathophysiology of portal hypertension: Increased resistance to portal flow at the level of hepatic sinusoids is caused by: Intrahepatic vasoconstriction due to decreased nitric oxide production, and increased release of endothelin-1 (ET-1), angiotensinogen, and eicosanoids Sinusoidal remodeling disrupting blood flow.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of portal hypertension in cirrhotic patients is explained by various factors that include decreased tone of the hepatic vasculature from endothelial dysfunction due to disequilibrium between vasoconstrictors and vasodilators and increased hepatic resistance to portal blood flow from cellular damage, fibrosis, and regenerative nodular formation. [12] When the HVPG increases beyond a certain threshold level, usually ≥ 10 mmHg, portal hypertension becomes clinically significant, resulting in complications such as ascites, development of gastroesophageal varices, hepatic encephalopathy, and functional renal failure. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 792
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties, a category of esophageal varices occurring at sites other than the gastro‑oesophageal junction, includes two distinct entities: دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف (esophageal varices without documented bleeding) and نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). This category is positioned under the broader parent term دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة (esophageal disease) and is also considered a type of esophageal disorders. In the hierarchical classification system, Other specified diseases of veins and lymphatics classifies both esophageal disorders and دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, indicating that these esophageal conditions are grouped with other specified vascular and lymphatic diseases. Clinically, esophageal disorders are associated with تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption), and alcohol use is likewise linked to دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, underscoring alcohol as a common risk factor for esophageal pathology. Moreover, esophageal disorders encompass the SMQ group الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (liver failure, cirrhosis, hepatic infiltration, and related liver‑damage conditions), which also includes دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة as a member. This relationship reflects the well‑recognized connection between chronic liver disease, portal hypertension, and the development of esophageal varices, including the risk of variceal bleeding. Together, these interrelated concepts highlight the classification, clinical associations, and pathophysiological links among esophageal varices, broader esophageal disorders, alcohol use, and underlying liver disease.

Varices of other locations are a subgroup of esophageal disorders and a child of دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, encompassing esophageal varices without bleeding (دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف) and esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء), which directly relates to esophageal disorders. The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2405
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties, a term describing varices that occur at sites other than the most common locations, includes as a subcategory نزيف دَوالِي المريء, which refers to bleeding from esophageal varices. This relationship underscores that variceal disease can affect diverse anatomical regions and that esophageal variceal hemorrhage represents a specific, potentially life‑threatening manifestation of varices outside the typical locations. Managing varices therefore requires awareness of possible bleeding complications such as نزيف دَوالِي المريء.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1804
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is associated with نزيف الدوالي الوريدية, which refers to variceal bleeding. This relationship indicates that bleeding from dilated venous varices—commonly occurring in the esophagus or stomach as a result of portal hypertension—is an important cause of gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Recognizing that نزيف الدوالي الوريدية can present as gastrointestinal hemorrhage is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management, including endoscopic hemostasis, vasoactive medications, and treatment of the underlying portal hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2273
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties is a broader classification of varicose lesions that encompasses a more specific subcategory, دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف. The subcategory دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف refers to varices occurring at various other anatomical sites for which no bleeding has been documented. This hierarchical relationship clarifies that varices of other locations can be further described by the presence or absence of bleeding, with دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف representing the subset where bleeding is not mentioned. Recognizing this distinction aids clinicians in accurately categorizing varicose conditions and tailoring appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: Esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices is a therapeutic endoscopic procedure used to treat دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices), which are located at المفاغرة المريئية (the esophageal junction) and represent a manifestation of وريد متدلي (dangling vein) within the spectrum of esophageal disorders. دوالي المَريء serve as a parent condition for نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding) and are pathophysiologically linked to دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة (intestinal varices) and varices van overige lokalisaties (varices of other locations), all sharing a common vascular component classified under Other specified diseases of veins and lymphatics. These venous dilations arise secondary to portal hypertension caused by chronic تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption) and underlying liver disease captured by الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (SMQ) (liver failure, cirrhosis, hepatic fibrosis, and related conditions). Consequently, دوالي المَريء and دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة are frequent sources of gastrointestinal hemorrhage, particularly upper‑GI bleeding, and require interventional strategies such as ربط دوالي المريء (band ligation) and other esophageal therapeutic procedures to achieve hemostasis. The clinical picture is further complicated by the association of chronic alcohol use with traumatic injuries such as closed head injury and ankle fracture, underscoring the need for integrated diagnostic and management approaches that address liver pathology, variceal disease, bleeding risk, and alcohol‑related morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1136
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are linked to the condition دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices) and are considered a member of the broader category Other specified diseases of veins and lymphatics. Within this framework, esophageal disorders also classify اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى (other esophageal disorders) and encompass the clinical syndrome الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (SMQ), which includes liver failure, cirrhosis, hepatic fibrosis and related hepatic injury. This hepatic disease group is further associated with دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة (intestinal varices), indicating that esophageal varices and intestinal varices frequently co‑occur. Varices van overige lokalisaties (varices of other locations) serves as a higher‑level category that includes both دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف (esophageal varices without mention of bleeding) and نزيف دَوالِي المريء (bleeding esophageal varices) as its child entities. This category is positioned under both esophageal disorders and دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, reflecting its role in connecting esophageal and intestinal variceal disease. The relationship “has relationship” between esophageal disorders and نزيف دَوالِي المريء underscores the clinical importance of bleeding as a key manifestation of esophageal varices. Overall, the knowledge structure highlights that esophageal disorders, esophageal varices, and intestinal varices are interrelated conditions within the spectrum of venous and lymphatic diseases, often occurring together and frequently linked to underlying severe liver pathology. Recognizing these connections aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with variceal disease and associated hepatic complications.

Varices of other locations (Varices van overige lokalisaties) are a subtype of gastric varices, are children of esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء), and are also placed under the broader category of esophageal disorders. Esophageal disorders are classified as other esophageal disorders (اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى) and are anatomically situated in the esophagus (ezofágus). They encompass liver‑related conditions such as liver failure, cirrhosis and other hepatic injuries (SMQ for liver failure, cirrhosis, etc.), which are members of دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, highlighting the link between portal hypertension, hepatic disease, and variceal formation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed “Operation on esophageal varices” is defined by a morphology that involves a وريد متدلي (dilated or hanging vein). The clinical condition نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding) is described as having a وريد متدلي as its parent structure, indicating that the bleeding originates from a dilated vein. Consequently, the وريد متدلي serves as the central anatomical element linking both the therapeutic intervention for esophageal varices and the pathogenesis of variceal hemorrhage, highlighting its significance in the diagnosis and management of esophageal vascular disorders.

This bleeding is a major cause of نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage) and is linked to نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding). Overall, the framework links esophageal variceal pathology, its underlying vascular morphology, associated bleeding manifestations, alcohol‑related liver disease, portal hypertension, and the endoscopic therapeutic procedures used for diagnosis, classification, and patient‑centered management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 810
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is categorized as a form of upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) and frequently presents with esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء). It is closely associated with variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية), highlighting the clinical importance of identifying variceal manifestations when evaluating and managing patients with gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 717
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء), dilated veins (وريد متدلي) located at the esophageal junction (مفاغرة مريئية), are classified under “Other specified diseases of veins and lymphatics” and belong to the broader category Varices van overige lokalisaties (varices of other locations). They co‑occur with intestinal varices (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة) and together form subtypes of venous varices, with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء) and bleeding of venous varices (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية) as child entities. Clinically, alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) is strongly linked to alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) and other liver‑damage conditions (SMQ) such as liver failure (الفَشَل الكَبِديّ), fibrosis (التَلَيُّف) and cirrhosis (تشَمُّعُ الكَبِد, تشَمُّع كَبِدي). These hepatic disorders give rise to portal hypertension (الضغط الوريدي البابى) which is a core component of the SMQ group and precipitates bleeding of esophageal varices, a form of upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage (نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي, gastrointestinal hemorrhage). Management of esophageal varices includes esophageal therapeutic procedures (إجراءات علاجية خاصة بالمريء) such as band ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and interventions at the esophageal junction. Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are frequently observed in patients with chronic alcohol use and alcoholic cirrhosis, reflecting the systemic impact of these conditions.
